blog.title: Building a price comparison app with Typescript

content.intro: There is nothing better than finding your favorite product at a better price with little effort. This motivated me to develop this web application that fetches the prices from various Bolivian online stores to easily compare the results.
content.info: This project and the guide were made for learning purposes, and the code is available on Github.
content.purpose: In this post, I'll explain how you can develop a similar project with Google Images search results using React, Fastify, and Typescript.

definitions.title: Some important concepts first
definitions.answer1: Web scraping involves extracting information from websites automatically. The basic idea is to obtain the HTML code of a website, identify the data using a programming language, and convert it into an easy-to-use format such as JSON.
definitions.answer2: React is a Javascript library used to build user interfaces with JSX. We'll use it to display the results from Google and the search bar.
definitions.answer3: Fastify is a framework used to create high-performance APIs with NodeJS. We'll use it as a server to web scrap data and send the results to our React app client.

step2.explanation: Currently, it doesn't do anything, but it demonstrates the essence of web scraping. The function <0>{{command1}}</0> fetches the HTML code from Google and converts it into an object of type <0>{{command2}}</0>.
step2.implementation: Every website is different, but with the help of <0>{{library1}}</0>, we can retrieve the data we need. For our Google Images example, the code for the above functions is as follows:
step2.codeexplanation: The first function simply makes a GET request to a website and returns its HTML code as a string. The second function parses that string into an usable DOM object, allowing us to use CSS selectors to obtain the image links and titles of all the results found.

step3.title: Create a Fastify endpoint
step3.laststep: The final step with the backend is to register a new route in <0>{{command1}}</0>. This route will return the results from Google when we make GET requests to the route <0>{{command2}}</0>.
step3.info: Remember to restart the Fastify server to use the new route.

step5.title: Displaying the images
step5.plan: To display the images, the following steps are required:

steps.0: Create a state variable for the search value.
steps.1: Create a state variable for the products.
steps.2: Create a function that calls our Fastify server's web scraping endpoint.
steps.3: Link that function to the search button.

step5.results: If everything went well, the results should be displayed after clicking the search button.

conclusion.title: Last words
conclusion.summary: In this case, it was pretty simple to create a web scraper and display the results in a React application.
conclusion.others: But other websites may require logging in, solving captchas, or the website might block suspicious activity. However, all of this is possible using more advanced web scraping techniques, so good luck!
